5. Time-Dependent Perturbation Theory (TDPT, Ch 27)

5.1. Interaction Picture

  • We mentioned in 8.05 that the unitary operator evolving wavefunctions is given by a nested integral of time ordered products of the Hamiltonian.
  • A time-depedent (TD) Hamiltonian does not have energy eigenstates, and trying to set time-dependent energies does not work. In general, there is no system of recovering energy eigenstates.

  • We will model TD Hamiltonians as a known, time-independent Hamiltonian plus a TD perturbation . We make no assumptions on the “smallness” of for now:

    (50)

  • By completeness, the general solution will be a time-dependent linear combination of the energy eigenstates of .

Let be the time evolution operator of starting from (Note. we can define it this way regardless of initial conditions).

where

Example 2 (Nuclear magnetic resonance). We can write the Hamiltonian for a particle with magnetic moment inside a magneteic field as , where is the Larmor angular velocity, a function of the magnetic field. Consider a longitudinal magnetic field with a rotating transverse field that rotates at the Larmor frequency of the longitudinal field. The system is at resonance and has a simple solution using the interaction figure.

5.4. Constant Perturbations (27.4)

We can describe a time-independent perturbation as turning on the pertubation at and turning it off at . “An analysis based on time-independent perturbation theory would be more cumbersome. We would have to first find the new spectrum, then express the initial Ĥ(0) eigenstate in terms of the new eigenstates, perform time evolution, and, finally, reexpress the result in terms of Ĥ(0) eigenstates.”

Using Equation 59, we obtain

(60)

Note that , and most of is contained within an interval of width

We can consider two classes of transmissions: energy conserving () and nonconserving :

  1. Energy conserving: We get , yielding
  2. Nonconserving transitions: We get . This oscillates in time, with amplitude . Thus the larger the energy difference the smaller the probability of transition.

5.5. Harmonic perturbations (27.5)

Suppose is periodic in time with some frequency , i.e.

(61)

Using our general formula for the transition amplitude from an intial state to , we get

(62)

Mathematically, the first term dominates for (), and the second term dominates for (). These correspond to emission and absorption respectively. For absorption, we get

(63)

We have two conditions for validity:

  1. Condition to neglect one of the terms over the other: We need the separation of and to be large compared to the width of the main lobe is given by , i.e. must include a number of periods of the wave so that the perturbation is identified as oscillatory..
  2. Validity of perturbation theory: We must have , requiring .

Combining the two bounds, for a suitable range of to exist, we need to have , i.e. the matrix element of the perturbation has energy must smaller than the energy separating the two levels.

Remark. Since absorption and emission have frequencies that only differ by a sign, a wave of frequency can drive both absorption and emission processes.

5.6. Fermi’s Golden Rule (27.6)

Fermi’s golden rule applies to transitions where the initial state of the system is part of a discrete spectrum, but the final state is part of a continuum of states. For example, the ionization of a hydrogen atom starts from a discrete bound state, while the final state includes a free electron which follows a continuum of momentum eigenstates.

We need to count the continuum states, so we replace infinite space by a very large cubic box of side length , and we will impose periodic boundary conditions on the wavefunctions. “While any reasonable boundary condition should work, periodic boundary conditions are the simplest to implement”. The effect of the box should go to zero as . This will discretize the continuous spectrum where the separation of between states is infinitesimal. The discrete part is negligibly affected for large . See Zwiebach Figure 27.8

  • Normalized momentum eigenstates take the form .
  • Quantization condition yields where
  • Define the density of states as . We have , so .
  • For a very large box, summing over states is equivalent to integrating over .

We are interested in the probability of a transition to a continuum state Gn time . We will consider constant and harmonic perturbations separately.

5.6.1. Constant Perturbations

  • For small and large , the transition probability is quadratic and linear, respectively. We can’t apply this to too large such that the first-order transition probabilities become large and unreliable.

Fun fact Dirac came up with the Dirac delta function during this derivation.

  • Linear dependence implies we get define a transition rate , or probability of transition per unit time.

Theorem 5 (Fermi's Golden Rule). The scattering rate per unit solid angle is

(64)

where is a continuum state of energy in the orientation .

  • For constant perturbations, . For harmonic perturbations, we get two scattering rates, for absorption and transmission: .
  • Need to integrate over ? Nontrvial if we assume its not spherically symmetric. https://piazza.com/class/m6p8qxxkemq5i4/post/98

Remarks on validity:

  1. We assume is large enough so the energy range is narrow so the factor is approximately constant over the range . Denote this energy range as .
  2. Simultaneously it cannot be arbitrarily large so that perturbation theory breaks down.

Combining the two conditions yields an intermediate time range . This interval exists if . Substituting the golden rule we get . By definition, we have so we get , or equivalently .

  • Fermi’s golden rule: We get the scattering rate per unit solid angle is where corresponds to absorption and emission respectively and is a continuum state of energy .
  • The condition for validity is the same as above except we evaluate the expressions at .

5.7. Example: Helium Atom and Autoionization (27.7)

Let’s discuss an example of where Fermi’s golden rule applies. Energy levels of helium ignoring Coulomb repulsion between the electrons is The least energy continuum state is when nad approaches infinity. This continuum appears for as the free electron can have arbitrary positive kinetic energy. Another continuum starts at and so on.

Add in Coulomb repulsion as a constant perturbation that produces “autoionizing” transitions to the continuum. For example the where both electrons are in the bound state may transition to the continuum. It turns out that radiative transition from to is a lot less likely than the autoionizing transition from to and the other electron being ejected. In general this is known as the Auger effect (filling of an inner-shell vacancy is accompanied by the emission of an electron instead of photon).

[TODO] We can use Fermi’s golden rule for constrant transitions to calculate the lifetime of the state.

Definition 5. Autoionization is a process where a molecule spontaneously emits one of the outer-shell electrons. Autoionizing states are usually short-lived.

5.8. [TODO] Decay for large : Wigner-Weisskopf Model (27.8)

Let’s model the decay of a discrete state to the continuum for large . Fermi’s golden rule predictes the probability of not decaying as . We will show that we get an exponential .

See iPad notes for a diagram.

“The Wigner–Weisskopf model illustrates that while energy is conserved overall (when including all products and fields), the energy of an unstable state is inherently uncertain due to its finite lifetime. In other words, although Fermi’s golden rule in the infinite-time limit appears to enforce energy conservation strictly via a delta function, the decay process described by the Wigner–Weisskopf model reveals that the decaying state has a natural energy width (typically a Lorentzian profile). This width arises because the state’s finite lifetime, as dictated by the energy-time uncertainty principle, means that its energy is not precisely defined. The observed “leakage” into neighboring energies reflects this uncertainty rather than an actual violation of energy conservation. Energy conservation is maintained when accounting for the entire system (including emitted particles), but the individual unstable state spreads out in energy due to its decay dynamics.”

5.9. Ionization of Hydrogen (27.9)

Let’s illustrate the use of Fermi’s golden rule by calculating the ionizatino rate for hydrogen when hit by a linearly polarized plane eletcromagnetic wave. The hydrogen atom has its electron on the ground state, and in the ionization process a photon ejects the bound electron.

  • Dipole assumption.
  • Ignore magnetic forces since the magnetic forces are weaker by a factor of .
  • Coulomb gauge plus aside
  • Computing inner product of harmonic oscillation
  • Use Fermi’s golden rule to derive scattering rate per solid angle. integrate over that to obtain total scattering rate (27.9.43).

(65)

  • Validity condition from FGR works out to be .

5.10. Atoms and Light, Spontaneous Emission (27.10, 27.11)

We also expect transitions between discrete levels from the harmonic oscillation, obtaining absorption and emission (no need to apply Fermi’s golden rule since the final state is discrete). Absorption is intuitive. The emission means that the incoming photon stimulates emission and does so without being absorbed, thus emerging accompanied by a second photon. This stimulated emission is how lasers work1.

[TODO] Photon produced from stimulated emission is in same direction as incident photon

Where is the spontaneous emission process that we know from chemistry? Einstein used a statistical argument to show the need for spontaneous emission.

  • Define and so that and are the rates of absorption and stimulated emission, respectively. Define so that is the rate of spontaneous emission.

Proof.

  • Consider a photon bath …
  • We expect from Hermiticity of first order perturbation theory.

(66)

Remarks.

  • The origin of spontaneous emission is stimulated emission by the vacuum fluctuations of the electromagnetic field.
  • Stimulated emission dominates spontaneous emission at high temperature when there are more photons at the right energy. Spontaneous emission dominates at low temperatures.

Let’s try to derive from first principles. As mentioned above, trying to compute would require us thinking about the transition stimulated by the vacuum fluctuations of the electromagnetic field. Luckily we have a formula for in terms of .

5.11. Selection Rules (27.12)

The decay rate between an initial and final state vanishes if . Define the parity operator as sending to . We have

(70)

If and are parity eigenstates, then the matrix element vanishes if they have the same parity.

Proposition 8. The spherical harmonics have parity .

Thus in the dipole approximation the difference in must be an odd number, and so on. Using a commutator from our derivation of the strong field zeeman effect, we get must equal plus or minus . Intuitively the incident photon carries angular momentum.

Further selection rules show that we require . Summarize . There are no constraints on .
